title: Writing - Part 1: Recycling and Waste
description: Your class has been discussing ways to reduce waste and improve recycling rates.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the approaches in your notes. You should explain which approach you think would be most effective, giving reasons for your opinion.

approaches: Government regulations and fines for excess waste
Redesigning products to use less packaging
opinions: People will only change their behaviour if there are financial penalties for not doing so.
Teaching children about recycling at school creates lifelong habits.
The real responsibility lies with manufacturers, not consumers.

structure.introduction: Introduce the problem of waste and the two approaches you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Discuss the first approach (e.g., government regulations) and why it is effective.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the second approach (e.g., redesigning products) and how it addresses the root of the problem.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both approaches and state which you think would be most effective.

content.modelAnswer:
Waste and pollution are serious global problems that require urgent and coordinated action. Despite growing public awareness, landfills continue to fill up and plastic waste continues to pollute oceans and ecosystems. This essay will consider the role of government regulation and the importance of redesigning products to minimise waste.

Government regulation can be a powerful tool in changing behaviour. By imposing fines on companies and households that produce excessive waste or fail to recycle, authorities can create strong financial incentives for change. Countries that have implemented strict waste policies, such as deposits on plastic bottles, have seen significant improvements in recycling rates.

However, a more effective long-term solution may be to redesign the products themselves. Much of the packaging used in supermarkets and online shopping is unnecessary or difficult to recycle. If manufacturers were required to use sustainable materials and minimal packaging, the problem would be addressed at its source rather than managed afterwards.

In conclusion, while government regulations are a useful short-term measure, I believe redesigning products to reduce packaging is ultimately the most effective approach. Changing consumer behaviour is slow and difficult; changing what products are made of and how they are packaged tackles the root of the problem far more directly and efficiently.
